steps.title: Locate the Entry Point
steps.instruction: Inspect the attic with a flashlight during or just after rain. Look for water stains, mold, or wet insulation to trace the water trail back to its entry point.
steps.decision: Is the entry point visible in the attic?

steps.title: Inspect Flashing
steps.instruction: Check all metal flashing around chimneys, vents, skylights, and valleys. Look for lifted edges, cracks, or gaps in sealant.
steps.decision: Is the flashing damaged or missing sealant?

steps.title: Inspect Shingles
steps.instruction: Walk the roof safely and check for missing, cracked, curled, or blistered shingles. Pay special attention to areas above the interior water stain.
steps.decision: Are shingles missing or visibly damaged?

steps.title: Check Roof Valleys and Gutters
steps.instruction: Inspect the roof valleys for debris buildup and damaged valley flashing. Check gutters for blockages that could cause water to back up under shingles.
steps.decision: Is there debris or damage in the valley/gutter area?

steps.title: Water Test
steps.instruction: With a helper inside the attic, slowly run a hose over different roof sections from bottom to top, starting at the suspected area. Wait several minutes per section.
steps.decision: Did the helper observe water entry during the hose test?

resolution_paths.action: Clear gutters and repair valley flashing
Engage licensed roofing contractor for full inspection

steps.title: Verify Scope
steps.instruction: Turn on multiple faucets throughout the house. Determine if low pressure is isolated to one fixture, one area (hot or cold only), or house-wide.
steps.decision: Is the low pressure isolated to one fixture?

steps.title: Check the Pressure Regulator
steps.instruction: Locate the pressure reducing valve (PRV) near the main shutoff. Attach a pressure gauge to an outdoor hose bib. Normal is 40-80 psi. Below 40 psi indicates PRV or supply issue.
steps.decision: Is pressure below 40 psi at the main?

steps.title: Inspect Shutoff Valves
steps.instruction: Confirm the main shutoff valve and any secondary shutoffs are fully open. A partially closed valve is a common cause of sudden pressure drops.
steps.decision: Are all shutoff valves fully open?

steps.title: Check for Mineral Buildup
steps.instruction: Remove aerators from affected faucets and clean them. Inspect showerheads for mineral deposits. For galvanized pipes, buildup may require pipe replacement.

steps.title: Test the Outlet
steps.instruction: Plug a known-working device into the dead outlet. Also test adjacent outlets. Use a non-contact voltage tester to confirm whether voltage is present at the outlet.
steps.decision: Does the voltage tester detect power at the outlet?

steps.title: Check GFCI Outlets and Breaker
steps.instruction: Locate any GFCI outlets in the same bathroom, kitchen, garage, or exterior. Press the RESET button firmly on all GFCI outlets. Also check the main panel for a tripped breaker (switch in middle position).
steps.decision: Did resetting the GFCI or breaker restore power?

steps.title: Inspect Wiring at Outlet
steps.instruction: Turn off the breaker for that circuit. Remove the outlet cover and outlet from the box. Inspect wires for loose connections, burn marks, or broken wires.
steps.decision: Are wires loose or is there visible burn damage?

decision_points.question: GFCI or breaker reset fixed it?

resolution_paths.action: Reset GFCI or reset circuit breaker
Re-secure wire connections and replace outlet

steps.title: Verify Temperature and Power
steps.instruction: Check thermostat setting (should be 37°F fridge / 0°F freezer). Confirm the unit is plugged in and the compressor is running (listen for hum at the back bottom).

steps.title: Inspect Condenser Coils
steps.instruction: Pull the refrigerator away from the wall. Locate condenser coils at the back or underneath. Check for heavy dust buildup which restricts heat dissipation.
steps.decision: Are condenser coils heavily coated with dust?

steps.title: Check Door Seals
steps.instruction: Close the door on a piece of paper. If the paper pulls out easily, the door gasket is failing and warm air is entering. Inspect the full perimeter of both doors.
steps.decision: Is the door gasket failing the paper test?

steps.title: Test Start Relay
steps.instruction: Unplug the fridge. Pull the start relay off the compressor side terminal. Shake it next to your ear. A rattling sound indicates a failed relay.
steps.decision: Does the start relay rattle or fail continuity test?

resolution_paths.action: Clean condenser coils with brush and vacuum
